title: The Perils of Running Your Own Mastodon Instance
summary: So I've been on the fediverse for about a year now. I started on mastodon.social but held off on self-hosting until just a few weeks ago. content:
So I've been on the fediverse for about a year now. I started on mastodon.social but held off on self-hosting until just a few weeks ago. I thought it was gonna kill my poor server to federate so much (I've heard things about Ruby on Rails, not all positive), so I just didn't. Eventually, I was pushed over into running my own instance literally because I was bored and thought the digital sovereignty would be cool and all.

I first tried setting up Pleroma, but I couldn't really figure out how to have it work with OpenID and how to block federation to less-than-savoury instances. So, I bit the bullet and installed Mastodon. Gripes about git clone being the recommended way to run Mastodon in production aside, I ran into no issues installing and configuring Mastodon! First things first, I blocked a lot of those aforementioned instances. I do not want to see CSAM or hate in my feed. That brought me to the first issue:

Finding bad instances is hard

My first idea for finding bad instances was to go to an established instance and look at the blocked instance list. That got me a solid list of instances to block, but the ones that I wanted to block the most had their domains censored. Finding public blocklist that is up-to-date was an exercise in frustration, but I eventually found one. Some sed magic and a bash script later, I imported the blocklist with curl. Great! Thing is, sometimes...

Stuff leaks through

Every now and then, as I trawl the federated timeline or some of the comments to a post, I see something that makes me do a little double-take. I click on the post, then the user, then check the local timeline of the server. And I think to myself...

Oh god there's so much hate on the internet

and I lose faith in humanity just a little. I check the user, I look at the admin, I look at the local timeline, and at that point I've been ingesting concentrated hate for the last twenty minutes. I block that instance and forget about that.

But then it happens again.

And again.

And again.

And it's up to me, as the sole moderator (and user) of my single-user instance to keep my own timeline clear.

And it's exhausting.

And I begin to understand the amount of pain professional moderators go through.

And I understand just a little more what it means to be an underpaid TikTok content moderator.

But I'm not gonna stop

Because, in my opinion, the benefits of running your own server outweigh the downsides. I have control over what I post on the internet. I'm not being tracked. I can trust my own judgement. People who are wary of users from ultra-large instances are willing to talk to me. I've built a deeper and stronger network.

But, with the increase of users leaving other websites and moving to the fediverse, my job is gonna become a little harder.

Time will tell, I guess.

Hey! You forgot to mention that running a super-small fedi instance also has xyz downsides!

Yeah it's kinda annoying that I can't see the number of boosts / favourites on a post.

What about the technical issues?

Updating Mastodon is hard, but I don't really mind the downtime. After all, I'm the only user.

Can I follow you on Mastodon?

Sure! I'm @ersei@ersei.net.

As always, if there is anything that I missed or that I have wrong, feel free to contact me.
